comment_content:
There were quite a few funny moments in this documentary - and that chef was hilarious! However, I do wish the documentary covered some of the real dangers the passengers faced - as well as the potential dangers they were lucky enough to avoid. As bad as things were, things could've gotten MUCH worse for them. This doc mainly focused on the passengers who were horrified over the inconveniences they faced .... but what about the passengers who felt vulnerable and afraid because they recognized the potential for tragedy on a drifting, incapacitated ship?

Imagine being stuck on a cruise ship after knowing there'd been a large fire onboard - and then losing access to electricity, running water, and the ability to communicate with the outside world. Surely many of these passengers had worries that went beyond toilet access and the lack of air conditioning. What if a secondary fire broke out on the ship? If so, could it even be extinguished? Were the fire pumps hooked up to emergency generators - or did they require power from the ship's engines to work? Hopefully, it was the former because without working pumps to deliver water to fire hoses or the sprinkler system, there'd be no way to keep a fire from spreading through a ship and consuming everything in its path. Sure, these people were on a floating city surrounded by water, but all that water would've been useless if the pumps didn't work. Also, the ship was listing to one side, so did that mean the (bilge?) pumps that would ordinarily kept the ship from taking on too much water weren't functioning? Surely there were passengers on board who were very concerned about the ship capsizing. For those people, getting free booze was probably the last thing they cared about.

Being stuck on a floating, tilting petri dish/potential firetrap with a finite supply of unspoiled food and drinking water should've been the real nightmare for these people - not the indignity of having to relieving oneself in a receptacle other than a toilet. (That would've been the least of my worries if I'd been a passenger!) Making the documentary about petty, spoiled Americans who refused to poop in bags and had obviously never faced a moment of hardship in their lives detracted from the seriousness of the situation they were in. Just because there were no icebergs in sight didn't mean there was no danger. These passengers may have thought they were stuck in a "worst case scenario", but things absolutely could've gotten much, much worse for them. They're lucky no other fires broke out on the ship - or they didn't get stuck in a violent storm - or there weren't 20+ foot waves beating their (already listing) ship and threatening to capsize it - or the ship didn't drift into shallower waters where a reef could've torn a hole in its hull.

comment_content: I was on this cruise and I'm glad to see some of my footage made the film, except, they didn't give credit. To answer some questions I see, Netflix had to stick to their theme. They chose people to interview who would also stick to their theme about it just being about poo. They left out a lot. The worst part was the danger and Carnival didn't tell us much at all while on the ship. We had no idea if everyone survived the fire or not and many people weren't getting food. People mostly slept under the lifeboats because Carnival was not being honest and we listed the entire time (tilted ship). While all this was happening, we were getting different stories from the crew ect. When situations like this happen, a lot of it is uncertainty. It's easy to watch this and know everything that's going on because they are telling you, but in the moment, we had no idea, we woke up to smoke everywhere and knew there was a serious fire and Carnival was lying left and right...that makes a very intense situation. The problem with pooing in bags was that we also didn't have running water until the last few days of 5. So it was go without washing your hands or anything for that matter, or don't go.
reply_content: Thank you for sharing your story ❤ Cant imagine how it was being there

People mostly slept under the lifeboats because Carnival was not being honest and we listed the entire time (tilted ship). While all this was happening, we were getting different stories from the crew ect. When situations like this happen, a lot of it is uncertainty. It's easy to watch this and know everything that's going on because they are telling you, but in the moment, we had no idea, we woke up to smoke everywhere and knew there was a serious fire and Carnival was lying left and right...that makes a very intense situation. The problem with pooing in bags was that we also didn't have running water until the last few days of 5. So it was go without washing your hands or anything for that matter, or don't go.
